meta.title: AWS-DEA-8: Data Engineering Operations and Governance

questions.stem: A company needs to ingest clickstream data from a website at 100,000 events per second with sub-second latency for real-time analytics. Which AWS service is best suited for this ingestion layer?

options.text: Amazon SQS
AWS Database Migration Service (DMS)
Amazon S3 with event notifications
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Amazon Kinesis Data Streams best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Amazon SQS does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Streams.

**B** is incorrect. AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Streams.

**C** is incorrect. Amazon S3 with event notifications does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Streams.
questions.reference: [What is Amazon Kinesis Data Streams? - Amazon Kinesis Data StreamsWhat is Amazon Kinesis Data Streams? - Amazon Kinesis Data Streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/introduction.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue ETL job fails with OutOfMemoryError when processing a 500 GB Parquet file from S3. The job uses DynamicFrames. What is the most effective solution?

options.text: Switch from DynamicFrames to RDD operations
Use Glue Streaming instead of batch processing
Enable job bookmarks to skip already-processed records
Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Switch from DynamicFrames to RDD operations does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job.

**B** is incorrect. Use Glue Streaming instead of batch processing does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job.

**C** is incorrect. Enable job bookmarks to skip already-processed records does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Increase the number of DPUs (data processing units) allocated to the Glue job.
questions.reference: [AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/author-job.html)

questions.stem: You need to process streaming data from Kinesis Data Streams, apply transformations, and deliver results to Amazon Redshift and Amazon S3 simultaneously. Which service handles fan-out delivery with built-in transformations?

options.text: Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka)
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose)
Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics
AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose) best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose).

**C** is incorrect. Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose).

**D** is incorrect. AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose (Amazon Data Firehose).
questions.reference: [What is Amazon Data Firehose? - Amazon Data FirehoseWhat is Amazon Data Firehose? - Amazon Data Firehose](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/what-is-this-service.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job reads from a JDBC source and writes to S3 in Parquet format. After several months, you notice that the number of output Parquet files is very large (500,000+ small files). How should you reduce the file count?

options.text: Enable Glue job bookmarks to consolidate output files
Call coalesce() or repartition() on the DynamicFrame before writing to reduce the number of Spark partitions
Use the Glue merge operation to compact files post-write
Enable S3 multipart upload to combine files during write

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Glue DynamicFrames are backed by Spark DataFrames. Using `.toDF().coalesce(N)` (or `.repartition(N)`) reduces the number of Spark output partitions before writing, directly controlling the number of output files. For example, `coalesce(100)` outputs approximately 100 Parquet files.

**A** is incorrect. Job bookmarks track which data has been processed to avoid reprocessing; they do not consolidate output files.

**C** is incorrect. Glue merge is used for merging Iceberg/Delta/Hudi tables with upsert semantics, not for compacting plain Parquet output files.

**D** is incorrect. S3 multipart upload is a mechanism for efficiently uploading individual large files; it does not combine multiple small output files into one.

[AWS Glue Developer Guide – Reducing the number of output files](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-partitions.html)
questions.reference: [Managing partitions for ETL output in AWS Glue - AWS GlueManaging partitions for ETL output in AWS Glue - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-partitions.html)

questions.stem: You need to replicate changes from an on-premises Oracle database to Amazon S3 in near real-time using change data capture (CDC). Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.text: AWS DataSync
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a custom Oracle listener
AWS Glue with JDBC connection polling every 5 minutes
AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. AWS DataSync does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled.

**B** is incorrect. Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a custom Oracle listener does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled.

**C** is incorrect. AWS Glue with JDBC connection polling every 5 minutes does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) with CDC enabled.
questions.reference: [What is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration ServiceWhat is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dms/latest/userguide/Welcome.html)

questions.stem: Which AWS service provides a serverless SQL interface to query data directly in Amazon S3 without loading it into a database?

options.text: AWS Glue DataBrew
Amazon EMR
Amazon Athena
Amazon Redshift

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Amazon Athena best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. AWS Glue DataBrew does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Athena.

**B** is incorrect. Amazon EMR does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Athena.

**D** is incorrect. Amazon Redshift does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Athena.
questions.reference: [What is Amazon Athena? - Amazon AthenaWhat is Amazon Athena? - Amazon Athena](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/what-is.html)

questions.stem: A data engineering team uses AWS Glue to transform data. They want to implement unit testing for Glue ETL scripts locally before deploying to AWS. Which TWO approaches support local testing of Glue scripts? (Select TWO)

options.text: Amazon EMR Serverless with Glue job templates
AWS Glue Interactive Sessions in a Jupyter notebook
AWS Glue local development libraries (aws-glue-libs) with Docker
Testing directly in the AWS Glue console with live production data

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. AWS Glue Interactive Sessions provide a notebook-based development experience connected to a serverless Glue session, enabling iterative development and testing without a full job run.

**B** is correct. AWS Glue provides `aws-glue-libs` as a Docker image for local development — you can run and test Glue PySpark scripts on a local machine without connecting to AWS.

**C** is incorrect. Testing in the production console with live production data is a bad practice that risks corrupting production data and incurring unexpected costs.

**D** is incorrect. EMR Serverless is a separate service and does not support running Glue scripts natively — Glue and EMR Serverless use different runtime environments.

[AWS Glue Developer Guide – Local development](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-libraries.html)
questions.reference: [Developing and testing AWS Glue job scripts locally - AWS GlueDeveloping and testing AWS Glue job scripts locally - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-libraries.html)

questions.stem: You are using Amazon Data Firehose to deliver JSON records to S3. You want records to be automatically converted to Parquet and partitioned by year/month/day without writing custom code. Which Firehose feature provides this?

options.text: Firehose dynamic partitioning with data format conversion (Apache Parquet via AWS Glue schema)
An AWS Lambda transformation function that converts JSON to Parquet before delivery
An S3 lifecycle policy that converts JSON files to Parquet after delivery
AWS Glue ETL job triggered by S3 event notification after Firehose delivery

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Amazon Data Firehose supports native data format conversion (JSON to Parquet or ORC) using a schema defined in AWS Glue Data Catalog. Dynamic partitioning allows extracting key values from JSON records and using them to partition S3 output (e.g., `year=2024/month=01/day=15`). Both features are built-in to Firehose, requiring no Lambda functions or post-processing jobs.

**B** is incorrect. A Lambda transformation function can modify record content (e.g., filter or enrich), but it cannot natively convert JSON to columnar Parquet format — Firehose's built-in format conversion does this without Lambda.

**C** is incorrect. S3 lifecycle policies manage object transitions (Standard to Glacier) and expiration, not format conversion.

**D** is incorrect. Triggering a Glue ETL job after delivery adds latency and operational overhead — Firehose's built-in conversion eliminates the need for a separate job.

[Amazon Data Firehose – Data format conversion](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/record-format-conversion.html)
questions.reference: [Convert input data format in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data FirehoseConvert input data format in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data Firehose](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/record-format-conversion.html)

questions.stem: Your data lake on S3 has grown to 5 PB with files from multiple years. Most queries access only the last 90 days of data. How should you configure S3 to minimize storage costs for older data?

options.text: Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year
Delete objects older than 1 year
Enable S3 Requester Pays for older data
Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all objects

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**B** is incorrect. Delete objects older than 1 year does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year.

**C** is incorrect. Enable S3 Requester Pays for older data does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year.

**D** is incorrect. Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for all objects does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Create S3 lifecycle policies to transition objects to S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days and Glacier Deep Archive after 1 year.
questions.reference: [Managing the lifecycle of objects - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceManaging the lifecycle of objects - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lifecycle-mgmt.html)

questions.stem: An Amazon Redshift cluster experiences slow query performance on a table with 500 million rows. EXPLAIN plan shows data redistribution between nodes. The table is queried with joins on customer_id. What should you do?

options.text: Change the sort key to customer_id
Enable result caching for all queries
Set the distribution style to ALL for the table
Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Change the sort key to customer_id does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node.

**B** is incorrect. Enable result caching for all queries does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node.

**C** is incorrect. Set the distribution style to ALL for the table does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Set the distribution key (DISTKEY) to customer_id to co-locate join data on the same node.
questions.reference: [Distribution styles - Amazon RedshiftDistribution styles - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c_choosing_dist_sort.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement a data lakehouse on AWS with support for ACID transactions, time travel queries, and schema evolution on S3-backed tables. Which table format should you use?

options.text: AWS Lake Formation governed tables
Plain Parquet files with Glue Data Catalog
Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog
Amazon Redshift external tables via Redshift Spectrum

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. AWS Lake Formation governed tables does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog.

**B** is incorrect. Plain Parquet files with Glue Data Catalog does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog.

**D** is incorrect. Amazon Redshift external tables via Redshift Spectrum does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Apache Iceberg or Apache Hudi with Glue Data Catalog.
questions.reference: [Using the Iceberg framework in AWS Glue - AWS GlueUsing the Iceberg framework in AWS Glue - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/aws-glue-programming-etl-format-iceberg.html)

questions.stem: An Amazon DynamoDB table stores user sessions with TTL set on each item. The table uses on-demand capacity mode. Queries filter on a non-key attribute (last_active_date). Performance is poor for these queries. What should you do?

options.text: Enable DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) for all read operations
Create a Global Secondary Index (GSI) with last_active_date as the partition key
Switch to provisioned capacity mode with auto-scaling
Use DynamoDB Streams to export data to Athena for analytical queries

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. DynamoDB requires queries to use the primary key or a secondary index. Filtering on a non-key attribute (`last_active_date`) requires a full table scan, which is slow and expensive. A Global Secondary Index (GSI) with `last_active_date` as the partition key allows efficient queries on that attribute without scanning the entire table.

**A** is incorrect. DAX improves read latency for point lookups (GetItem, BatchGetItem) and caches query/scan results, but it does not fix the underlying scan caused by missing an index on `last_active_date`.

**C** is incorrect. The capacity mode (on-demand vs. provisioned) affects how throughput is managed, not query patterns. Switching to provisioned does not add an index.

**D** is incorrect. DynamoDB Streams + Athena export is useful for analytical workloads on historical data, but it does not solve real-time query performance on the live DynamoDB table.

[Amazon DynamoDB Developer Guide – Global Secondary Indexes](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/GSI.html)
questions.reference: [Using Global Secondary Indexes in DynamoDB - Amazon DynamoDBUsing Global Secondary Indexes in DynamoDB - Amazon DynamoDB](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/GSI.html)

questions.stem: Which Amazon Redshift feature allows you to query data directly in S3 without loading it into Redshift, using the same SQL interface?

options.text: Redshift Spectrum

questions.reference: [Amazon Redshift Spectrum - Amazon RedshiftAmazon Redshift Spectrum - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c-using-spectrum.html)

questions.explanation:

**A** is incorrect. Amazon Aurora does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility).

**B** is incorrect. Amazon ElastiCache does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility).

questions.reference: [What is Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility) - Amazon DocumentDBWhat is Amazon DocumentDB (with MongoDB compatibility) - Amazon DocumentDB](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/documentdb/latest/developerguide/what-is.html)

questions.stem: You need to orchestrate a multi-step data pipeline that includes Glue jobs, Lambda functions, and EMR jobs with dependencies between steps. Failures should trigger retries. Which AWS service is designed for this?

options.text: AWS Batch
Amazon EventBridge scheduled rules
AWS Step Functions
Amazon SQS with Lambda triggers

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. AWS Step Functions best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. AWS Batch does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Step Functions.

**B** is incorrect. Amazon EventBridge scheduled rules does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Step Functions.

**D** is incorrect. Amazon SQS with Lambda triggers does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Step Functions.
questions.reference: [What is Step Functions? - AWS Step FunctionsWhat is Step Functions? - AWS Step Functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/welcome.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job uses job bookmarks to track processed S3 files. After fixing a bug in the transformation logic, you need to reprocess all files from the beginning. What should you do?

options.text: Delete and recreate the Glue job
Reset the Glue job bookmark to clear the bookmark state, then run the job
Disable job bookmarks and re-enable them after reprocessing
Modify the job's MaxRetries setting to force full reprocessing

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. AWS Glue job bookmarks store the state of what has been processed so that subsequent runs only process new data. To reprocess all data, you reset the bookmark state via the Glue console (Actions > Reset job bookmark) or via the AWS CLI (`aws glue reset-job-bookmark`). After reset, the next job run processes all available data from the beginning.

**A** is incorrect. Deleting and recreating the Glue job is unnecessarily destructive — it would lose any configuration, triggers, and run history. Bookmark reset achieves the same goal without destroying the job.

**C** is incorrect. Disabling job bookmarks would prevent the next run from tracking state, but the old bookmark state is not cleared — re-enabling would resume from the last bookmark position, not from the beginning.

**D** is incorrect. MaxRetries controls how many times a failed job run is automatically retried, not whether previously processed data is reprocessed.

[AWS Glue Developer Guide – Tracking processed data using job bookmarks](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/monitor-continuations.html)
questions.reference: [Tracking processed data using job bookmarks - AWS GlueTracking processed data using job bookmarks - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/monitor-continuations.html)

questions.stem: Your team runs multiple Apache Spark jobs on Amazon EMR for daily data processing. Jobs occasionally fail due to spot instance interruption. What should you configure to make the cluster resilient to spot interruptions at lowest cost?

options.text: Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only
Use Spot instances for all nodes including master and core
Enable EMR Serverless which automatically handles instance interruptions
Use only On-Demand instances for all EMR node types

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**B** is incorrect. Use Spot instances for all nodes including master and core does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only.

**C** is incorrect. Enable EMR Serverless which automatically handles instance interruptions does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only.

**D** is incorrect. Use only On-Demand instances for all EMR node types does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Use On-Demand instances for the master and core nodes; use Spot instances for task nodes only.
questions.reference: [Configuring Amazon EMR cluster instance types and best practices for Spot instances - Amazon EMRConfiguring Amazon EMR cluster instance types and best practices for Spot instances - Amazon EMR](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/emr/latest/ManagementGuide/emr-plan-instances-guidelines.html)

questions.stem: A daily ETL pipeline fails intermittently. CloudWatch Logs show the Glue job completing successfully, but downstream Athena queries return stale data. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: S3 eventual consistency is delaying object visibility to Athena
Glue DynamicFrames do not flush buffered writes to S3 immediately
Athena result caching is returning old query results from a previous execution
The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. S3 eventual consistency is delaying object visibility to Athena does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE.

**B** is incorrect. Glue DynamicFrames do not flush buffered writes to S3 immediately does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE.

**C** is incorrect. Athena result caching is returning old query results from a previous execution does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The Glue Data Catalog table was not updated after writing new S3 partitions; run a Glue Crawler or call MSCK REPAIR TABLE.
questions.reference: [Using crawlers to populate the Data Catalog - AWS GlueUsing crawlers to populate the Data Catalog - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/add-crawler.html)

questions.stem: You need to monitor an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream and automatically scale the number of shards based on incoming data volume. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Kinesis shards are fixed and cannot be scaled after stream creation
Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards
Create a CloudWatch alarm on the IncomingBytes metric and trigger a Lambda to call UpdateShardCount
Use Kinesis Scaling Utilities (open-source) deployed on EC2

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Kinesis shards are fixed and cannot be scaled after stream creation does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards.

**C** is incorrect. Create a CloudWatch alarm on the IncomingBytes metric and trigger a Lambda to call UpdateShardCount does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards.

**D** is incorrect. Use Kinesis Scaling Utilities (open-source) deployed on EC2 does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode which automatically scales shards.
questions.reference: [Choose the right mode to stream in - Amazon Kinesis Data StreamsChoose the right mode to stream in - Amazon Kinesis Data Streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/streams/latest/dev/how-do-i-size-a-stream.html)

questions.stem: You need to create a CloudWatch dashboard that shows the number of records processed per minute by a Kinesis Data Stream, Glue job DPU utilization, and S3 PUT requests. What metrics must you configure?

options.text: All metrics are automatically available; create a CloudWatch dashboard and add them as widgets
Kinesis and S3 metrics are in CloudWatch by default; Glue job metrics require enabling Glue job observability metrics
All three services require custom metric publishing via the CloudWatch Logs agent
S3 request metrics must be enabled at the bucket level; Kinesis and Glue metrics are automatic

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Kinesis Data Streams publishes metrics (IncomingRecords, GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds, etc.) to CloudWatch automatically. S3 request metrics (PutRequests, GetRequests) must be enabled per bucket under S3 > Metrics > Request metrics — they are not enabled by default. AWS Glue DPU utilization metrics require enabling the Continuous Job Metrics or Observability Metrics feature in the Glue job configuration.

**A** is incorrect. S3 request metrics and Glue detailed metrics are not enabled by default; they require explicit configuration.

**C** is incorrect. None of these services require custom CloudWatch Logs agent publishing — their metrics are published natively to CloudWatch once enabled.

**D** is incorrect. Option D is partially correct about S3 but incorrectly states that Glue metrics are automatic. Glue DPU utilization metrics require enabling observability metrics.

[Amazon S3 User Guide – Monitoring metrics with Amazon CloudWatch](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/cloudwatch-monitoring.html)
questions.reference: [Monitoring metrics with Amazon CloudWatch - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceMonitoring metrics with Amazon CloudWatch - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/cloudwatch-monitoring.html)

questions.stem: An S3 bucket stores sensitive customer PII data. Data engineers should be able to read and write objects, but must not be able to delete them. How do you enforce this using IAM?

options.text: Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject; omit s3:DeleteObject from the policy (implicit deny)
Enable S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode on the bucket
Use an S3 bucket policy that allows only the data engineer IAM role
Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject; omit s3:DeleteObject from the policy (implicit deny) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject.

**B** is incorrect. Enable S3 Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode on the bucket does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject.

**C** is incorrect. Use an S3 bucket policy that allows only the data engineer IAM role does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Grant s3:GetObject and s3:PutObject permissions; explicitly deny s3:DeleteObject.
questions.reference: [Policy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access ManagementPolicy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access Management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_evaluation-logic.html)

**C** is incorrect. AWS Glue Data Catalog with IAM resource policies does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS Lake Formation.

questions.stem: An AWS Glue job must read from an RDS database in a private VPC subnet (no public access). What configuration is required?

options.text: Enable RDS public accessibility and restrict access with a security group rule for the Glue IP range
Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet
Create a VPC endpoint for AWS Glue in the RDS VPC
Attach a NAT Gateway to the Glue job's network configuration

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Enable RDS public accessibility and restrict access with a security group rule for the Glue IP range does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet.

**C** is incorrect. Create a VPC endpoint for AWS Glue in the RDS VPC does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet.

**D** is incorrect. Attach a NAT Gateway to the Glue job's network configuration does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Configure the Glue job to use a Glue Connection with VPC, subnet, and security group settings pointing to the RDS subnet.
questions.reference: [AWS Glue connection properties - AWS GlueAWS Glue connection properties - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/connection-properties.html)

questions.stem: You need to ensure that sensitive data stored in S3 is automatically classified and that any unencrypted S3 buckets are detected and flagged for compliance. Which combination of AWS services covers both requirements?

options.text: Amazon Macie alone — it detects both sensitive data and unencrypted buckets
Amazon Macie for sensitive data classification and AWS Config for encryption compliance monitoring
AWS Security Hub alone — it aggregates all findings without needing separate services
AWS Config alone — it can classify sensitive data content and check encryption settings

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Amazon Macie uses machine learning to automatically discover and classify sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) in S3 buckets. AWS Config's `s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled` managed rule continuously evaluates whether S3 buckets enforce server-side encryption and flags non-compliant buckets. Using both provides comprehensive coverage: Macie for content-level sensitive data classification, AWS Config for encryption compliance.

**A** is incorrect. Amazon Macie is focused on sensitive data discovery and classification; while it reports on some bucket security posture findings, it is not the primary tool for encryption compliance monitoring across all buckets.

**C** is incorrect. AWS Security Hub aggregates and correlates findings from Macie, Config, GuardDuty, and other services, but it does not perform the underlying sensitive data discovery or encryption compliance checks — it relies on the individual services to produce those findings.

**D** is incorrect. AWS Config evaluates resource configuration against compliance rules but does not inspect the content of S3 objects to classify sensitive data.

[Amazon Macie User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/what-is-macie.html)
questions.reference: [What is Amazon Macie? - Amazon MacieWhat is Amazon Macie? - Amazon Macie](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/what-is-macie.html)

questions.stem: A Redshift cluster query logs contain SQL statements that include raw customer email addresses. You need to ensure that sensitive data is masked in query results. Which approach implements this?

options.text: Encrypt the Redshift cluster at rest with KMS
Use Redshift column-level access control to restrict email column access
Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption for Redshift audit logs
Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Encrypt the Redshift cluster at rest with KMS does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns.

**B** is incorrect. Use Redshift column-level access control to restrict email column access does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns.

**C** is incorrect. Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption for Redshift audit logs does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable Redshift Dynamic Data Masking policies on sensitive columns.
questions.reference: [Dynamic data masking - Amazon RedshiftDynamic data masking - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/t_ddm.html)

questions.stem: You need to build a real-time data pipeline that reads from a Kinesis Data Stream, enriches each event by looking up reference data in DynamoDB, and writes results to S3. Which service provides the lowest operational overhead?

options.text: Amazon EMR Streaming with Spark Structured Streaming
Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation
Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink)
AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Amazon EMR Streaming with Spark Structured Streaming does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation.

**C** is incorrect. Amazon Kinesis Data Analytics (Apache Flink) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation.

**D** is incorrect. AWS Lambda with Kinesis trigger does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose with Lambda transformation.
questions.reference: [Transform source data in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data FirehoseTransform source data in Amazon Data Firehose - Amazon Data Firehose](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/firehose/latest/dev/data-transformation.html)

questions.stem: An Amazon Redshift table query takes 30 minutes. EXPLAIN shows a step with DS_DIST_ALL_INNER, indicating one table is redistributed to all nodes for every join. The redistributed table has 10 million rows and is 500 MB. What is the recommended fix?

options.text: Enable Concurrency Scaling to handle the redistribution load
Set DISTSTYLE EVEN on the large redistributed table
Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently
Increase the Redshift cluster node count

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Enable Concurrency Scaling to handle the redistribution load does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently.

**B** is incorrect. Set DISTSTYLE EVEN on the large redistributed table does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently.

**D** is incorrect. Increase the Redshift cluster node count does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Set DISTSTYLE ALL on the 500 MB table so it is replicated to all nodes permanently.
questions.reference: [Distribution styles - Amazon RedshiftDistribution styles - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c_choosing_dist_sort.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement data quality checks on incoming S3 data before it is processed by Glue. Checks include: null count threshold, value range validation, and uniqueness. Which AWS service provides managed data quality rules for Glue data?

options.text: AWS Glue Data Quality (DQDL rules)
Amazon DataZone data quality rules
Custom Lambda function with Great Expectations library
AWS Config with custom rules for S3 data validation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. AWS Glue Data Quality uses the Data Quality Definition Language (DQDL) to define declarative quality rules (ColumnValues, IsUnique, RowCount, etc.). Rules can be applied to Glue tables or embedded in Glue ETL jobs as a pre/post-processing step. Results are stored as quality scores and can fail the job or trigger alerts. This is the native AWS approach for Glue-based data quality.

**B** is incorrect. Amazon DataZone is a data management and governance service focused on data discovery, cataloging, and sharing across business domains — it does not provide Glue-integrated DQDL data quality rules.

**C** is incorrect. A custom Lambda with Great Expectations is a valid open-source approach, but it adds operational overhead of maintaining Lambda code and third-party library dependencies compared to the native Glue Data Quality feature.

**D** is incorrect. AWS Config evaluates AWS resource configurations against compliance rules — it is not designed to validate the content of data files in S3.

[AWS Glue Data Quality](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/glue-data-quality.html)
questions.reference: [AWS Glue Data Quality - AWS GlueAWS Glue Data Quality - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/glue-data-quality.html)

questions.stem: An AWS Glue Spark job processes 10 TB of data daily. After optimizing the Spark code, average job duration is still 4 hours. The data is read from S3 in Parquet format with 400,000 small files (25 MB each). What single change would most reduce job duration?

options.text: Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes
Enable Glue Auto Scaling to dynamically adjust DPUs
Switch from Parquet to ORC format for better compression
Increase the number of DPUs to 200

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**B** is incorrect. Enable Glue Auto Scaling to dynamically adjust DPUs does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes.

**C** is incorrect. Switch from Parquet to ORC format for better compression does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes.

**D** is incorrect. Increase the number of DPUs to 200 does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Compact the small Parquet files into larger files (e.g., 512 MB each) and update the S3 prefixes.
questions.reference: [Best practices design patterns: optimizing Amazon S3 performance - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceBest practices design patterns: optimizing Amazon S3 performance - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/optimizing-performance.html)

questions.stem: Your organization requires that all data in S3 used by the data lake be encrypted with customer-managed keys that you can rotate annually. Which encryption configuration satisfies this requirement?

options.text: Use client-side encryption with your own key before uploading to S3
Enable S3 Block Public Access and encrypt using SSE-C
Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-S3 (AES-256)
Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK)

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK) best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Use client-side encryption with your own key before uploading to S3 does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK).

**B** is incorrect. Enable S3 Block Public Access and encrypt using SSE-C does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK).

**C** is incorrect. Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-S3 (AES-256) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Enable S3 default encryption with SSE-KMS using a customer-managed KMS key (CMK).
questions.reference: [Using server-side encryption with AWS KMS keys (SSE-KMS) - Amazon Simple Storage ServiceUsing server-side encryption with AWS KMS keys (SSE-KMS) - Amazon Simple Storage Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html)

questions.stem: Amazon Athena queries on a partitioned S3 table are scanning more data than expected, even though partition pruning should apply. The partition filter is applied using a WHERE clause. What could cause this?

options.text: The Glue Crawler set the wrong data type for the partition column
The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning
Athena does not support partition pruning
The S3 bucket versioning is enabled, causing Athena to scan all object versions

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. The Glue Crawler set the wrong data type for the partition column does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning.

**C** is incorrect. Athena does not support partition pruning does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning.

**D** is incorrect. The S3 bucket versioning is enabled, causing Athena to scan all object versions does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The WHERE clause uses a function on the partition column (e.g., YEAR(date_col) = 2024) preventing pruning.
questions.reference: [Partition your data - Amazon AthenaPartition your data - Amazon Athena](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/partitions.html)

questions.stem: You need to transfer 50 TB of data from an on-premises data center to Amazon S3 over a dedicated 10 Gbps AWS Direct Connect connection. Approximately how long will the transfer take at maximum throughput?

options.text: Approximately 11 hours
Approximately 2 days
Approximately 5 days
AWS Direct Connect cannot be used for S3 data transfer

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. 50 TB = 50 × 1024 × 8 Gbits = 409,600 Gbits. At 10 Gbps: 409,600 / 10 = 40,960 seconds ≈ 11.4 hours at full theoretical throughput. In practice, usable throughput is 60–80% of nominal, so expect 14–18 hours. The exam tests the theoretical maximum calculation, which gives approximately 11 hours.

**B** is incorrect. Two days (48 hours) would correspond to roughly 2.4 Gbps average throughput — far below the 10 Gbps Direct Connect capacity.

**C** is incorrect. Five days would imply less than 1 Gbps average throughput, which is not representative of a dedicated 10 Gbps Direct Connect link.

**D** is incorrect. AWS Direct Connect is absolutely used for S3 data transfer via the S3 gateway endpoint or virtual private interface.

[AWS Direct Connect User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html)
questions.reference: [What is Direct Connect? - AWS Direct ConnectWhat is Direct Connect? - AWS Direct Connect](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html)

questions.stem: A Step Functions state machine orchestrates a data pipeline. One state makes an API call to a third-party service that takes 20-30 minutes to complete. The state machine should wait for the result without polling. Which Step Functions pattern should you use?

options.text: Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete
Parallel state with a 30-minute timeout
Express Workflow with synchronous execution
Task state with a polling loop (Wait + Choice state)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**B** is incorrect. Parallel state with a 30-minute timeout does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete.

**C** is incorrect. Express Workflow with synchronous execution does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete.

**D** is incorrect. Task state with a polling loop (Wait + Choice state) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Task state with .waitForTaskToken, where the third-party service calls back with the token when complete.
questions.reference: [Discover service integration patterns in Step Functions - AWS Step FunctionsDiscover service integration patterns in Step Functions - AWS Step Functions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/connect-to-resource.html)

questions.stem: You need to perform time travel queries on a Delta Lake table stored in S3 to recover data that was accidentally deleted. You are using Amazon Athena. What must be true to support this?

options.text: Enable S3 Versioning on the Delta table bucket and use the S3 console to restore previous file versions
Delta Lake time travel requires Databricks Runtime; it is not available on AWS
Athena does not support Delta Lake time travel; use Amazon EMR with Spark instead
The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Enable S3 Versioning on the Delta table bucket and use the S3 console to restore previous file versions does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax.

**B** is incorrect. Delta Lake time travel requires Databricks Runtime; it is not available on AWS does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax.

**C** is incorrect. Athena does not support Delta Lake time travel; use Amazon EMR with Spark instead does not best satisfy the requirement compared with The Delta table transaction log (_delta_log) must be intact, and Athena must query using the VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF syntax.
questions.reference: [Query Linux Foundation Delta Lake tables - Amazon AthenaQuery Linux Foundation Delta Lake tables - Amazon Athena](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/athena/latest/ug/delta-lake-tables.html)

questions.stem: You need to audit all API calls made to your S3 buckets and Glue Data Catalog for compliance purposes, including who accessed what data and when. Which AWS service provides this audit trail?

options.text: AWS Config
Amazon CloudWatch Logs
AWS CloudTrail
S3 Server Access Logs

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. AWS CloudTrail best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. AWS Config does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS CloudTrail.

**B** is incorrect. Amazon CloudWatch Logs does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS CloudTrail.

**D** is incorrect. S3 Server Access Logs does not best satisfy the requirement compared with AWS CloudTrail.
questions.reference: [What Is AWS CloudTrail? - AWS CloudTrailWhat Is AWS CloudTrail? - AWS CloudTrail](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-user-guide.html)

questions.stem: You want to be notified when an AWS Glue job fails within 5 minutes of the failure. Which configuration achieves this?

options.text: Create a CloudWatch alarm on the glue.driver.jvm.heap.used metric with an SNS notification
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that matches Glue job state change events with FAILED status and triggers an SNS topic
Enable Glue job email notifications in the Glue console notification settings
Configure an S3 event notification on the Glue logs bucket

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. AWS Glue publishes job state change events to Amazon EventBridge automatically. Creating an EventBridge rule with the pattern `{source: 'aws.glue', detail-type: 'Glue Job State Change', detail: {state: ['FAILED']}}` and targeting an SNS topic delivers near-immediate failure notifications. This is event-driven — no polling delay — and is the recommended AWS pattern for Glue job monitoring.

**A** is incorrect. The `glue.driver.jvm.heap.used` metric reflects JVM memory usage, not job failure state. A CloudWatch alarm on this metric would alert on memory pressure, not job failures.

**C** is incorrect. There is no built-in Glue console email notification setting for job failures — EventBridge + SNS is the correct native approach.

**D** is incorrect. S3 event notifications trigger on object creation or deletion in S3 buckets — not on Glue job state changes. The Glue job log files being written to S3 would trigger on every log batch, not specifically on job failure.

[Amazon EventBridge – Events from AWS services](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eventbridge/latest/userguide/eb-service-event.html)
questions.reference: [Amazon EventBridge](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eventbridge/latest/userguide/eb-service-event.html)

questions.stem: You need to migrate a 10 TB MySQL database from on-premises to Amazon Aurora MySQL with minimal downtime. The database serves an application that cannot tolerate more than 30 seconds of downtime during the final cutover. Which approach is best?

options.text: Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source
Use AWS DataSync to replicate MySQL binary log files to S3, then load into Aurora
Create an Aurora Read Replica of the on-premises MySQL and promote it
Stop the application, run mysqldump, transfer the dump to Aurora, restore, then start the application

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**B** is incorrect. Use AWS DataSync to replicate MySQL binary log files to S3, then load into Aurora does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source.

**C** is incorrect. Create an Aurora Read Replica of the on-premises MySQL and promote it does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source.

**D** is incorrect. Stop the application, run mysqldump, transfer the dump to Aurora, restore, then start the application does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Use AWS DMS with full load followed by CDC replication; cut over when Aurora is caught up to within seconds of the source.
questions.reference: [What is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration ServiceWhat is AWS Database Migration Service? - AWS Database Migration Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dms/latest/userguide/Welcome.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement an Amazon Redshift cluster that serves both interactive BI queries from analysts and bulk nightly ETL loads. Both workloads must not interfere with each other. Which Redshift feature isolates these workloads?

options.text: Create separate Redshift clusters for BI and ETL
Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling for BI queries
Redshift Query Monitoring Rules (QMR)
Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. Create separate Redshift clusters for BI and ETL does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs.

**B** is incorrect. Enable Redshift Concurrency Scaling for BI queries does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs.

**C** is incorrect. Redshift Query Monitoring Rules (QMR) does not best satisfy the requirement compared with Redshift Workload Management (WLM) with separate queues for BI queries and ETL jobs.
questions.reference: [Workload management - Amazon RedshiftWorkload management - Amazon Redshift](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c_workload_mngmt_classification.html)

questions.stem: A data engineer's IAM role has s3:GetObject permission on a specific S3 bucket. An S3 bucket policy explicitly denies s3:GetObject for all principals. Can the data engineer read objects from this bucket?

options.text: It depends on whether the IAM role has an attached permissions boundary
Yes, the IAM role allow takes precedence over the bucket policy deny
No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies
Yes, if the data engineer is the bucket owner

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies best matches the requirement described in the scenario.

**A** is incorrect. It depends on whether the IAM role has an attached permissions boundary does not best satisfy the requirement compared with No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies.

**B** is incorrect. Yes, the IAM role allow takes precedence over the bucket policy deny does not best satisfy the requirement compared with No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies.

**D** is incorrect. Yes, if the data engineer is the bucket owner does not best satisfy the requirement compared with No, explicit denies in any policy always override allows in other policies.
questions.reference: [Policy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access ManagementPolicy evaluation logic - AWS Identity and Access Management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_evaluation-logic.html)

questions.stem: You need to implement data lineage tracking for your AWS data lake, showing how data flows from source S3 buckets through Glue ETL jobs to final Redshift tables. Which AWS service provides native lineage tracking?

options.text: AWS CloudTrail with Athena analysis
Amazon DataZone
AWS Glue Data Catalog with lineage enabled
AWS Glue with Apache Atlas integration

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Amazon DataZone provides data lineage visualization showing how data flows through your AWS data estate. It integrates with Glue, Redshift, Athena, and S3 to automatically capture and display lineage graphs.

**A** is incorrect. CloudTrail captures AWS API calls and can be analyzed with Athena, but this provides an audit log of API activity, not a data lineage graph showing how data transforms and flows between systems.

**C** is incorrect. The AWS Glue Data Catalog stores metadata (database/table/schema definitions) but does not natively visualize or track data lineage graphs showing data flow between sources, transformations, and targets.

**D** is incorrect. Apache Atlas is an open-source data governance platform that can be integrated with Glue, but this requires additional infrastructure and is not a native AWS managed service.

[Amazon DataZone User Guide](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/datazone/latest/userguide/what-is-datazone.html)
questions.reference: [What is Amazon DataZone? - Amazon DataZoneWhat is Amazon DataZone? - Amazon DataZone](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/datazone/latest/userguide/what-is-datazone.html)
